does: This paper systematically evaluates Large Language Models as automated graders for short-answer questions using rubric-based evaluation. The authors assess three critical dimensions of LLM-judge performance using the SciEntsBank benchmark and Qwen 2.5-72B: alignment with expert judgment across rubrics of varying complexity, the relationship between prediction confidence and accuracy via a consensus-based deferral mechanism they call the Trust Curve, and robustness to random perturbations and adversarial attacks. Key findings show that LLM alignment with human experts is strongest for binary classification tasks but degrades as rubric granularity increases, that filtering low-confidence predictions improves accuracy on remaining cases, and that robustness varies under different types of input perturbations.
connects: This work builds on longstanding research in automated short-answer grading, which has traditionally used symbolic NLP and machine learning approaches to handle the linguistic variability and partial-credit complexity of student responses. It extends recent work applying LLMs to educational assessment tasks by providing the first rigorous, multi-faceted evaluation of LLM reliability in rubric-aligned settings. The paper connects to literature on calibration and uncertainty quantification in neural models, incorporating ideas from selective prediction and confidence-based deferral that have been explored in classification tasks. It also engages with robustness testing of language models, drawing on adversarial attack and perturbation evaluation methods developed in broader ML safety and reliability research.
enables: This work enables more trustworthy deployment of LLM-based grading systems in educational technology by establishing benchmarks and failure modes. The Trust Curve analysis and deferral mechanism point toward hybrid human-AI grading systems where LLMs handle confident predictions while humans review uncertain or complex cases, improving both efficiency and accuracy. The robustness findings identify specific vulnerabilities that future work can address through better prompting, ensemble methods, or fine-tuning approaches. More broadly, the framework for evaluating alignment, uncertainty, and robustness in rubric-conditioned tasks generalizes beyond grading to other high-stakes assessment and evaluation domains where nuanced, explainable AI judgment is required.
